hook: A 1950s pasha's courthouse hiding sixty rooms of zellij and carved cedar
facts: Built 1941-1952 for the pasha Tayeb El Mokri to designs by Auguste Cadet
Served as civil courthouse, governor's residence and parliamentary reception hall
Over sixty rooms of zellij, carved stucco and cedar ceilings behind a plain green-tiled exterior
Still a working administrative building, open to visitors when not in session
w: Behind a sober green-tiled wall in the Habous, sixty-odd rooms erupt into zellij, carved stucco and cedar ceilings, all raised in the 1940s for the pasha as courthouse, reception hall and jail at once. French architect, Moroccan craftsmen, colonial theatre in tilework. Knock and ask; the door sometimes opens.

hook: A 'new medina' the French invented from scratch in Mauresque style
facts: Laid out from 1917 by Albert Laprade and Henri Prost during the Protectorate
Built to rehouse traders as Casablanca outgrew the cramped old medina
Arcaded lanes, small squares and souks in a French-imagined Neo-Moorish idiom
Known for booksellers, brassware and the city's olive and pastry shops
w: French planners drew a Moroccan medina from a blank sheet in the 1920s: arcaded lanes, neat squares and souks in a tidied Neo-Moorish style, built to rehouse traders priced out of the old town. Too symmetrical to be truly old, too lived-in to be fake. Buy olives and brass; argue about authenticity later.

hook: A bar built to be the film set Hollywood never shot in Casablanca
facts: Opened 2004 by former US diplomat Kathy Kriger inside a 1930s courtyard mansion
The 1942 film was shot entirely on Warner Brothers lots in California, never here
Recreates the piano bar, arches and brass lamps of the movie's Cafe Americain
Kriger ran it for fourteen years until her death in 2018
w: The movie never came near Morocco; it was shot on Warner lots in Burbank. So in 2004 a retired American diplomat, Kathy Kriger, built the bar that should have existed, arches and piano and all, inside a 1930s medina mansion. Pure simulacrum, knowingly so. Of all the gin joints, she made this one.

hook: A deconsecrated white concrete church you can climb, minarets for towers
facts: Designed by Paul Tournon, built 1930-1953 in Art Deco neo-Gothic concrete
Twin towers deliberately echo Moroccan minarets
Lost its congregation after independence and was deconsecrated in the 1970s
Now an empty exhibition hall; stairs climb the towers for rooftop views
w: Never actually a cathedral and no longer a church, this white concrete pile by Paul Tournon mixes Art Deco, neo-Gothic and twin towers that mimic minarets. The Catholic congregation drained away after independence; the pews went, the shell stayed. Climb the bare staircases for a view nobody prays to. Beautiful and empty.

hook: A brutal concrete box flooded with eight hundred square metres of stained glass
facts: Built 1954-1956 by architect Achille Dangleterre in raw concrete
Walls hold roughly 800 square metres of dalle de verre glass by Gabriel Loire
Among the largest stained-glass programmes in North Africa
Still an active Roman Catholic church serving Casablanca's small congregation
w: From outside, a grey concrete shed; inside, walls dissolve into eight hundred square metres of thick slab glass by Gabriel Loire, the story of the Virgin told in burning blues and reds. One of the largest such programmes in North Africa, set into raw 1950s concrete. Brutal box, luminous heart. Go on a bright morning.

hook: The only Jewish museum in the Arab world, in a former orphanage
facts: Founded 1997 in a 1948 building that once housed a Jewish orphanage
Holds Torah scrolls, costumes, jewellery and reconstructed synagogue interiors
Morocco held the largest Jewish community in the Muslim world, some 250,000 before 1948
Fewer than 2,500 Moroccan Jews remain today
w: The Arab world holds exactly one Jewish museum, and it sits in a quiet Oasis villa that was once an orphanage. Inside: Torah scrolls, embroidered costumes, a whole reconstructed synagogue from a community that numbered a quarter-million before 1948 and barely two thousand now. A vanished world, carefully kept.

hook: A collector's Art Deco villa stuffed with Orientalist posters and Berber gold
facts: Opened 2012 in a 1940s Art Deco villa over three floors
Built around businessman Abderrahman Slaoui's private collection
Holds Morocco's largest set of Orientalist travel posters and 18th-19th century gold jewellery
Includes crystal, painting and a rooftop tea cafe
w: One man's magpie hoard, shown in his own 1940s Art Deco villa: the country's largest run of Orientalist travel posters, cases of 18th-century gold jewellery saved from the melting pot, crystal and Majorelle canvases. Slaoui collected obsessively; the result is small, dense and personal. Finish with mint tea on the roof.

hook: A 1930 Art Deco picture palace where Piaf and Josephine Baker played
facts: Built 1929-1930 by French architect Pierre Jabin, seating around 1,350
Hosted Edith Piaf, Charles Aznavour and Dizzy Gillespie on its stage
Josephine Baker performed here for American troops after Operation Torch in 1943
Run by the Bouchentouf family since the 1950s; still standing in the deco quarter
w: A 1930 Art Deco hall that once pulled Piaf, Aznavour and Dizzy Gillespie to its stage, and where Josephine Baker sang for GIs after the 1943 landings. Casablanca's last great picture palace, run by one family since the fifties, now flickering between screenings and slow decline. Sit under the geometric ceiling and listen for ghosts.

hook: A 1917 covered market behind a gate copied from imperial city walls
facts: Built 1917-1919 by Pierre Bousquet on the site of a 1915 trade fair
Entered through a Neo-Mauresque gate imitating Fes and Marrakesh portals
Iron-and-glass galleries shelter fishmongers, florists and produce stalls
Lunchtime grills cook the morning's catch to order
w: Step through a gate aping the great portals of Fes and Marrakesh and you land among iron-and-glass galleries from 1917, banked with fish, flowers and produce. At noon the grills fire up and the morning's catch goes straight to a plate. A French market hall in Moorish fancy dress. Come hungry, point at things.

hook: An 18th-century cannon bastion with its guns still aimed at the Atlantic
facts: Fortified gun platform built around 1769 under Sultan Sidi Mohammed Ben Abdallah
Rose on earlier Portuguese-era defences guarding the new port
Rusting bronze cannon still line the ramparts above the fishing harbour
Now wraps a walled garden and Moorish-style cafe
w: A row of bronze cannon still point seaward from this 1769 gun bastion, raised by Sultan Sidi Mohammed on older Portuguese defences to deter raiders from the young port. The guns rusted, the threat passed, and a walled garden and cafe grew up inside the ramparts. Order a tea where the powder once sat.

hook: The faded brasserie where Saint-Exupery drank between mail flights
facts: On Boulevard Mohammed V, frequented by Aeropostale pilots in the 1920s-30s
Antoine de Saint-Exupery flew the Casablanca-Dakar mail route and drank here
Albert Camus and Edith Piaf counted among later regulars
A side room keeps letters and aviator drawings on its walls
w: Between mail runs across the Sahara, the Aeropostale pilots drank here, Saint-Exupery among them, scribbling letters that later became books. Camus and Piaf passed through too. The brasserie has faded to brown nicotine glamour, a back room hung with aviators' drawings. Sit where 'Courrier Sud' was half-dreamed.

hook: A 210-metre minaret firing a nightly laser toward Mecca over the sea
facts: Completed 1993 by Michel Pinseau, partly built out over the Atlantic
Minaret stands 210 metres, the tallest in the world at its opening
A laser at the summit beams east toward Mecca, visible up to 30 km
Floor sections over the water let worshippers pray above the sea
w: Forget the postcard frontage; come after dark, when the 210-metre minaret, tallest on earth when it opened, fires a laser eastward across the city toward Mecca, throwing a thin red line thirty kilometres into the night. Below it, glass floor panels let the faithful pray above the breaking Atlantic. Engineering as devotion.

hook: A 1934 Art Deco mansion turned free contemporary art gallery
facts: Built 1934 by Diego Jimenez for the wealthy Toledano family
Cubic white Art Deco villa beside the Arab League Park
Run by a foundation as a free contemporary art museum since the 1990s
Gardens dotted with sculpture; admission costs nothing
w: A crisp white cube of 1934 Art Deco, raised for the Toledano family and now given over, for free, to Moroccan contemporary art. The villa itself rewards a slow look as much as the rotating shows inside it, and sculptures lurk in the garden. Park your eyes on the geometry, then on the paintings.

hook: A skeletal Neo-Moorish hotel that haunted the boulevard for decades
facts: Built 1916-1918 by Hubert Bride with rich Neo-Mauresque facades
Once the Hotel Lincoln, an emblem of early ville nouvelle Casablanca
Floors collapsed in December 1989, leaving a blackened ruin for years
Listed as a historic monument in 2000 and rebuilt as a hotel by 2025
w: For thirty years this Neo-Moorish hulk loomed over Mohammed V as a soot-black ruin, its floors caved in since a 1989 collapse, pigeons in the arches. Built 1916 as a proud hotel, listed as a monument while it rotted, finally resurrected. The city's most beautiful corpse, lately raised from the dead. Catch the before-and-after.

hook: An Art Deco block with shopping arcades built for the Pasha of Marrakesh
facts: Designed by Marius Boyer in 1922 for Thami El Glaoui, Pasha of Marrakesh
Among the first glass-roofed shopping arcades in Casablanca
Fuses Art Deco massing with Moroccan zellij detail
Stands near the foot of Boulevard Mohammed V
w: The Pasha of Marrakesh, Thami El Glaoui, wanted a slice of the boomtown, so Marius Boyer gave him this 1920s block with one of the city's first glass-roofed shopping arcades beneath it. Art Deco bones, Moroccan zellij in the detailing, old money cashing in on new. Duck into the passage for the faded grandeur.

hook: A concrete-jungle roundabout of deco oddities, lately the world's 2nd 'coolest' quarter
facts: Grew up around its roundabout in the early 1900s Protectorate boom
Lined with Art Deco, Art Nouveau and modernist apartment blocks
Landmarks include a liner-shaped cafe and a deco pharmacy sign
Ranked second-coolest neighbourhood worldwide by Time Out in 2022
w: Around one busy roundabout, Casablanca crams its deco strangeness: a cafe shaped like an ocean liner, a pharmacy sign in blue-and-white geometry, modernist blocks gone grey. Time Out crowned it the world's second-coolest neighbourhood in 2022, to local eye-rolls. Faded, loud, genuinely odd. Walk it slowly, look up constantly.
